frameworks.name: visual working memory prioritization
frameworks.one_line: Internal attention sets distinct priority states across stored memoranda.

frameworks.name: bidirectional memory-perception interactions
frameworks.one_line: Retroactive and proactive biases between memoranda and incoming probes.

frameworks.name: double-serial retro-cue paradigm
frameworks.one_line: Methodological frame forcing complete prioritization of one memorandum.

core_question: Does working memory prioritization modulate retroactive and proactive biases between memoranda and new visual inputs?
key_assumptions: Observers can shift internal attention toward task-relevant memoranda
Priority states map onto susceptibility to bidirectional report biases
Prior retrodictive cues confound prioritization incentives
manipulations: Prioritization state of two shape memoranda via first retro-cue
Independent second cue selecting report target (memory 1, memory 2, or probe)
Insertion of a perceptual probe between cues for comparison
key_findings: Robust retroactive and proactive report biases were observed
Only retroactive biases were modulated by prioritization
Prioritized memories were more precise with smaller attractive bias toward probe
Probe reports were biased comparably toward prioritized and unprioritized memories
interpretations: Prioritization asymmetrically governs memory-to-percept influence but not percept-to-memory influence
Priority states modulate susceptibility to retroactive interference selectively
